title: The functional role of PING: papers where the pyramidal-interneuron gamma mechanism is the vehicle for a functional claim
scope_paragraph: The corpus is papers in which the PING mechanism — gamma generated by reciprocal pyramidal-to-interneuron interaction, with its characteristic E-I timing — is explicitly the vehicle for a functional claim. A paper is IN if it both (a) invokes PING (or ING where explicitly contrasted as the competing E-I loop) as the generator of the gamma rhythm, and (b) argues that some cognitive/computational function follows FROM that mechanism. A paper is OUT if it is pure phenomenology (gamma correlates with a function, no generating mechanism named), pure mechanism with no functional claim (a sibling corpus), non-gamma, or the 'PING' networking-tool namesake.
purpose: Ground the lab's SNN / gamma-gating work in the literature where the E-I mechanism has to pay rent for a function, and produce an auditable, recall-measured corpus.

anchors.title: Gamma oscillations mediate stimulus competition and attentional selection in a cortical network model
Cortical Enlightenment: Are Attentional Gamma Oscillations Driven by ING or PING?
Rhythms for Cognition: Communication through Coherence

function_axes: coincidence detection / gain control / temporal window (concept-level, F2)

exclusions: pure phenomenology: gamma correlates with a function but no generating mechanism named
pure mechanism: PING generation studied with no functional claim (sibling corpus)
non-gamma oscillations (theta, beta, ripple) unless cross-frequency with a PING gamma claim
the 'PING' ICMP / network-latency tool namesake and unrelated 'PING' acronyms
loose communication-through-coherence papers that assume a gamma mechanism generically without naming the E-I/PING generator (F1: strict)

F1.question: CTC breadth
F1.decision: strict — only CTC papers that name PING/E-I as the generator

F2.question: coincidence-detection / gain axis
F2.effect: +~20-40, higher recall risk under other vocabulary

F3.question: reviews & opinion
F3.decision: keep — reviews that make the mechanism->function argument are corpus members

F4.question: era floor
F4.effect: back to Gray & Singer 1989 era gamma-synchrony-function papers

coverage_caveats: keyless run: OpenAlex + Crossref + arXiv reached; Semantic Scholar and PubMed used unauthenticated (throttled, reduced recall on biomed-indexed silos)
polite-pool contact email supplied to OpenAlex/Crossref
